Add 15-minute transition buffers before and after fixed obligations to account for setup, travel, and time-blindness-related drift.
Start both major assignments earlier in the week with explicit sub-deadlines for outline, draft, revision, and submission rather than relying on final-day completion.

scenarios.week_context: This is the final week before a major CS301 group project is due on Friday, and the student is juggling regular classes with multiple coordination meetings and last-minute integration work. Because they are an evening-type student with poor time perception, several important tasks have drifted into inefficient times of day and are packed too closely together, creating avoidable stress and transition problems.
expected_challenges: High-load project work is repeatedly scheduled during known trough hours (08:00-10:00 and 13:00-14:00), increasing distractibility and slow task initiation.
Several blocks exceed the student's max sustained focus of 20 minutes and are not decomposed, making hyperfocus crashes or avoidance more likely.
Transitions are packed too tightly, with multiple back-to-back lecture, meeting, and work sessions despite the student needing at least 15 minutes of buffer.
Time-blindness creates risk around deadline-sensitive coordination tasks like uploads, merge sessions, and team rehearsals, especially when they are scheduled late or after long work stretches.
Socially demanding group meetings are clustered with cognitively intense solo work, which may amplify anxiety and reduce follow-through on individual responsibilities.
optimal_interventions: Shift the most demanding individual project work into 16:00-20:00 peak hours and move admin or low-load tasks into trough periods.
Break all study and integration blocks into 25-minute work intervals with explicit 10-minute breaks and visible countdown timers.
Add 15-20 minute buffers before and after classes, meetings, and submission tasks to reduce transition friction and lateness.
Create earlier internal deadlines for file merging and submission, with one designated teammate owning final upload verification.

scenarios.week_context: The student has a major CS301 systems design assignment due Thursday at 23:59 after putting it off for nearly two weeks. They are now trying to cram the project into the next three days while still attending required classes, a lab, and a part-time work shift, leading to an overloaded and poorly timed week.
expected_challenges: High-load CS301 work is scheduled repeatedly during trough or fatigued hours, especially early mornings and immediately after long fixed commitments.
The assignment is not decomposed into smaller steps, making task initiation harder and increasing overwhelm for an inattentive student with high procrastination tendency.
Several study blocks exceed the student's max sustained focus of 25 minutes and lack adequate breaks or 15-minute transition buffers.
Late-night work sessions align with peak focus but likely push sleep later, worsening next-morning attendance and attention in fixed classes and lab.
Leaving submission until 23:00 on the due date creates risk from time blindness, perfectionism, and technical issues.
optimal_interventions: Break the CS301 assignment into visible sub-tasks with short 25-30 minute work sprints, each tied to a concrete deliverable like outline, code module, testing, and submission prep.
Move the most demanding CS301 tasks into evening peak hours and reserve morning trough periods for lighter admin, review, or class attendance only.
Insert protected meal breaks, 10-minute recovery breaks, and at least 15-minute transition buffers between classes, work, and study to reduce cognitive switching costs.
Set an internal submission deadline several hours before the real deadline, with a separate block for final upload and verification.

scenarios.week_context: The student is in the middle of a busy week with regular classes, a lab, and a part-time campus commitment while a large HIST210 research paper is suddenly only 3 days from deadline after being avoided until now. Because this student functions best in the morning but has packed several urgent paper sessions into the wrong parts of the day, the week creates a realistic emergency-cram situation with poor pacing and weak task decomposition.
expected_challenges: The HIST210 assignment is handled as several oversized study marathons instead of short decomposed sprints, which clashes with a 20-minute sustained focus limit.
Multiple high-load study blocks are scheduled during the student's known trough hours from 14:00 to 17:00, increasing restlessness, avoidance, and low-quality work.
There are too few buffers between cognitively demanding tasks despite the student needing at least 10 minutes to reset and transition.
Compressed meals and minimal recovery time after classes and lab raise the risk of impulsive schedule abandonment and inconsistent energy regulation.
Because time perception is low, the student may underestimate how long drafting, citation cleanup, and submission steps take and end up rushing the HIST210 deadline.
optimal_interventions: Break the HIST210 paper into explicit 25-minute components such as outline, source triage, thesis draft, body paragraph drafting, citations, and submission check, each with 5-minute breaks.
Move the highest-load independent work into 07:00-11:00 peak hours and reserve 14:00-17:00 for lower-load admin, reading review, walking, or collaborative tasks.
Insert protected 10-15 minute transition buffers and real lunch breaks between lecture, lab, and study sessions to reduce momentum loss and impulsive skipping.
Use external time anchors such as visible countdown timers, mini-deadlines, and a same-day submission checklist for the HIST210 paper to compensate for weak time perception.

scenarios.week_context: The student has a major CS301 systems project due Thursday night after putting it off for over a week, and is now trying to recover while keeping up with normal classes and a part-time campus job. Anxiety is high, so they keep reacting to urgency rather than planning well, leading to long cram sessions in poor time slots and not enough recovery between commitments.
expected_challenges: The student has multiple 90-180 minute high-load study blocks despite a maximum sustained focus of 35 minutes, making task avoidance, mind-wandering, and shutdown likely.
Several demanding tasks are scheduled during known trough hours (14:00-15:00 and 20:00-22:00), especially CS301 submission work and math study, which increases frustration and inefficient cramming.
There are too few transition buffers between classes, group work, lab, and study blocks, which may lead to lateness, skipped setup time, and difficulty switching attention.
High anxiety plus the looming deadline may trigger perfectionism and hyperfocus on the CS301 project, causing the student to neglect food, sleep, and other coursework.
Because the project work is not decomposed into smaller concrete steps, the student is more likely to freeze, underestimate time, and spend too long deciding what to do next.
optimal_interventions: Break the CS301 project into visible 30-40 minute sub-tasks with defined outputs such as outline, module fix, test pass, report draft, and final submission checklist.
Move the most cognitively demanding project and math work into the 10:00-12:00 peak window, and reserve trough hours for admin, lighter review, meals, or movement.
Insert 15-minute buffers and real breaks between major blocks, especially before lab, after lectures, and before evening work, to support transitions and reduce overload.
Create an emergency triage plan that identifies minimum viable completion for CS301, postpones nonessential tasks, and uses external accountability such as a professor email or study check-in.

Add required transition buffers and a firm lunch/dinner structure so the student does not slide from one demand into the next without recovery.
Create an earlier submission deadline on Thursday, with a dedicated final check block on Wednesday afternoon or Thursday morning to reduce last-minute risk.

The CS301 emergency work is not decomposed into small steps despite the student's 20-minute sustained focus limit, making it easier to freeze or procrastinate within the block.
Minimal buffers between classes, group work, and study sessions create transition friction and increase the risk of lateness or skipped tasks.
Multiple 2-4 hour cram sessions without planned breaks or meals raise the chance of hyperfocus, exhaustion, and time blindness around the deadline.
The schedule relies on late-night recovery work and inconsistent rest, which can worsen next-morning functioning and compound deadline anxiety.
